item.title: Staff Post - Rewrite
item.content:encoded: Honestly, not the first time we've had a picture that Wikipedia grossly mislabeled. For simplicity, changed it from One Step 600 to One Step Express. Thank you for pointing it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is not a "Polaroid One Step 600".
Here is a picture of a One Step 600 that is under CC 2.0 (compatible with 3.0), and I can even remove the "Business Edition" if needed.

Or we could change the text to say Iris has a One Step Express, for the sake of coherence.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: CC BY-SA 3.0
Title: Polaroid OneStep Express.jpg
Author: Retro00064
Release year: 2009

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: She probably wouldn't care at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what would happen if 105 found out her serial number puts her right next to an eldritch, ancient, incorporeal, acidic zombie rapist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, I feel like after five years my opinions on this have cooled down. Still not a fan of the melodrama in addendum 3 but such is life. My vote's unchanged for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one that wants to change the fact that the article calls the camera a Polaroid One Step 600 but the image is a Polaroid One Step Close Up 600, either change the picture to match the text, or change the text to match the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: haha, i literally played "life is strange" today. there's always an scp that references something i like, even if it's unintentional :D

item.title: Re: life is stange
item.content:encoded: I like this crossover potential.

item.title: life is stange
item.content:encoded: Are we allowed to?

item.title: Re: Stop Arguing. You're being immature. Stop that!
item.content:encoded: I mean, someone's being immature here, but I'm not sure it's who you're accusing of such.

item.title: Re: Stop Arguing. You're being immature. Stop that!
item.content:encoded:
That's racist, specist, anomacist, and psionicist, and you all know it.

No.

item.title: Re: Stop Arguing. You're being immature. Stop that!
item.content:encoded:
And about that X-Men thing? I don't think so.

When we use the term "X-men" with SCPs, we mean that the anomaly acts more like a traditional superpower than an anomalous property.

105, for better or worse, has sn anomaly that functions very much like a superpower, even if it's not flashy. Especially in light of the fact that she's associated with a MTF that may or may not be akin to an organization like the Justice League.

item.title: Stop Arguing. You're being immature. Stop that!
item.content:encoded: Disregard. I was an idiot back in the day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-105: Okay… My name is Iris Thompson, I was born in Phoenix, Arizona, on May 12th, ████.

As pointed out on the subreddit, it's Iris' birthday today!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I strongly support linking SCPs to popular stories about them. It would in fact be extremely unfair if Resurrection was the only canon, or even one of the few canons, getting linked in this way. I assume that's not going to be the case.

Proper framing is necessary, of course. Treats and Wayward Prince would be very appropriate as-is; Knee-Deep In The Keter would require some framing so as not to detract from 682 striking a serious tone.

I'd actually like to edit the crosslink to give it better framing (and will do so later), and I wouldn't mind tweaking the framing of it to also add a link to a sufficiently popular or well-done story contradicting Resurrection canon. Any suggestion is welcome. (Though I have no intention of adding a crosslink to a story written specifically to crap on Resurrection. Something that fits the spirit of 105, please.)

To respond briefly to a comment above:

Resurrection is already legitimized by virtue of its popularity, despite being the subject of a long-running (and at least temporarily somewhat effective) campaign to get people to stop writing it. This wasn't in any way a political move — it was done totally disconnected from current Resurrection writers, and the first I heard about this was today.

EDIT: I completely rewrote the end note, keeping the same crosslinks chosen by the Wikiwalk Team.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
why not link Knee-Deep in the Keter to 682? Treats to 106? The Wayward Prince to the Alexyva SCPs?

This actually sounds like a good idea. The tales are one of the most-neglected sections of the wiki, and linking the SCPs to the tales would only help their exposure. Since the purpose of the wiki-walk is to connect articles to each other, categorically ignoring the tales from the process to the SCPs can only be to the wiki's detriment.

Ultimately, the argument boils down to "I don't like Resurrection," and that's a selfish reason to prevent crosslinking. I'm not part of Resurrection and not even a particular fan of it, but it seems awfully clear that the two are very much thematically linked, and if someone is a fan of 105, they'll probably want to know about Resurrection. Without in-article links, the connection is not at all clear to the casual SCP browser.

Clef and Moose can change this if they so wish.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
If this article is popular with the same kinds of people who might like Resurrection, which seems to be the case, then I can't see linking as anything but a feature. If the goal is to encourage wiki-walks, linking things to related things is the way to do it.

The article already mentions Pandora's Box, so also mentioning Alpha-9 seems not dissimilar.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Completely disagree with this. To be fair I'm a writer for the canon in question, but WJS has (as far as I know) absolutely no connection to the Resurrection canon. This article, however, does. The current version of this skip was written by one of the most prolific writers for Resurrection and the founder of the canon.

There's no canon on this site, and linking this out to Resurrection doesn't somehow legitimize Ress over other canons. I'd imagine we'll be linking other canons from popular articles as well, since that's probably the best way to spread the love from those articles.

This article is not so highly rated, by the way, that I think it deserves enshrinement. Even if it was I'd have issues with that thinking, but it isn't. I don't think the "iconic" argument even applies (I'm sorry, but +221 isn't high enough to be iconic). If anything it's only iconic from the perspective of Resurrection, and probably sees a net benefit (despite a few vocal critics) from being associated strongly with that canon.

Ultimately, I very strongly think Clef and Moose's opinions on this change are the most important voices, as they are the co-authors. We've reverted, temporarily, to get their opinions. Whatever they want to do with their article is likely the course of action we'll follow.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Due to the objections to the wikiwalk edit that was made, it has been removed from the article until contact with the co-authors of this article can be established. Thank you all for your input though, we will keep it in mind when moving forward.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Won't really argue any of these if only because I have zero stake in the matter. However, I will say that the current incarnation of this draft was co-written by Clef & Moose (the version of this article prior to their rewrite is drastically different).

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the oldest, most iconic SCPs on the site, though— probably the only reason it's not more popular is because it hasn't been featured in something like Containment Breach as of yet. People are going to read this and see that it links to a canon. And then what happens? People are going to think that Resurrection is the be-all end-all of the SCP canon.

Almost half of the fandom doesn't give a crap about anything past 999, and almost half the fanbase gives no craps about anyone whose name doesn't start with "Dr." and end with a username. Series 1 is full of creepypasta (which the internet loves) and, let's face it, X-Men (which teenagers and young adults- which make up a good part of the fandom- can put themselves in the shoes of due to, let's face it, a rather bland characterization for most of them). Iris falls into the latter category, and, loathe as I am to admit it, an important part of site history.

Seeing Resurrection get legitimized in a popular, influential mainlist article like this reeks of an effort to make it the one true canon, a criticism that has been leveled at it much more eloquently in the past. It's reviving a part of site history that is only still popular because Iris, Cassie, Abel, Cain, 682, 953 and every single author avatar are non-character characters. They're essentially blank slates that people can see themselves being/hanging out with/doing unspeakable things to. There's a reason that more recent, stronger characters don't have as strong of a following— again, our fanbase is mostly teenagers and young adults, per the 2016 Community Survey, with almost 70% falling into the 15-24 age range, with 47% of that being from 15-19. This is the same age group most prominent on Fanfiction.net, per a 2010 survey.

In short, this whole thing reeks of a political move to try and make Resurrection the only thing on the site- "there is no canon" is going to be an extinct notion if this is kept up. And as for this point:

There are skips that link to canons already.

The SCPs and the canons they link to 1) usually have involvement from the original author of the SCP 2) are not iconic in the fanbase's eyes (no offense meant, but it's kind of true) and 3) are usually not written by the staff members in charge of the wiki. With the exception of the first point (as I understand it, the author of the current draft (who I think is moose?) allowed this) all of these are true of 105 and Resurrection.

But then again, it's 2:40 AM as I post this, and maybe I'm just tired and don't know what the fuck I'm talking about. Also, I will freely admit: Resurrection, to me, is probably one of the worst things on the site, so that bias should be taken into account.

Overall, though: this is a bad idea, a dangerous precedent, and in my eyes, the edit needs to be undone.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
There are skips that link to canons already.

Where were people when this was proposed?

EDIT: Actually, to be fair, I hardly can keep track of any of these proposals, let alone all of them, so I can't blame anyone for missing them. Disregard that question; I'm keeping it there for posterity.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Seconding this. Resurrection is not something that needs to be… well, resurrected. This has just earned a fresh downvote from me for the addition.

That cluster of a canon should be entirely separate from the mainlist. Crosslink the SCPs in the tales, don't reference the tales in the SCP. If we're going to do this, why not link Knee-Deep in the Keter to 682? Treats to 106? The Wayward Prince to the Alexyva SCPs? This sets a dangerous precedent, as it violates the "there is no canon" by forcing a canon into this.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Late, but I'd like to toss in an objection against this, as this is essentially retroactively editing series one skips to validate the Resurrection canon. And considering the size of said canon and the amount of exposure it already gets, I hardly think this particular cross-link is necessary.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Responding to myself to make it clear that a proposed WikiWalk adjustment is in place.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 72 hours if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

Adding the following addendum to connect SCP-105 to Resurrection:

Addendum 4: New Assignment to MTF Alpha-9

In the aftermath of the assault on Site-17, SCP-105 has been reevaluated as a potential force for use against hostile anomalous activity. After deliberation, it has been decided to reenlist her into MTF Alpha-9.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you think she's MTF material, you definitely have to read the Resurrection canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from minor tone issues bugging me, I really found myself liking this article. It's probably mainly me thinking "she sounds like mobile task force material" and the author thinking the same. She's also genuine enough as a character to not come across as an SCP-men like most humanoid SCPs of this type.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Addendum 3 was really somber, and kind of sad. Props to the writer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably, I just missed it in the article, but what is Iris' powers' limit, in terms of weight?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually think that the article itself is fine. The only thing I dislike about it is the note at the end. I don't generally like Omega-7 stuff, and the foundation should really have better discipline towards its employees than letting a doctor aid a SCP object in breaching containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Delayed response!

It's still supposed to be one task force, with multiple teams. That's true for any task force.

That said, something about that section has always rubbed me slightly wrong, but I've never been able to put my finger on why. I have editing privileges for this article. Any suggestions?

(Probably am not gonna cut Clef's phrase "Team Iris" at this point, but if there's a way to make the whole thing smoother, or even just parts of it, I'm open to hear it.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Responding to another post on another SCP, by sirpudding.

I think [Iris being classified as Safe is] an error, or at least an earlier version of object classes that predates the locked box test. Iris will die if you lock her in a box and forget about her. IMO living things require maintenance, and maintenance means Euclid.

For what it's worth, being one of the people responsible for the current rewrite (and therefore one of the people with permission to change the classification to Euclid if we saw fit), I think Iris is "Safe" for the reason of internal Foundation politics.

At one point, in the Bad Old Days, an SCP had to be Safe Class to be used by MTF Omega-7. This was, naturally, fucked up very quickly by reclassifying Euclid humanoids as Safe using political nudging, and re-codifying parts of the classification system to retroactively make that make sense, couched as "reforms".

(Out of universe, this literally did happen, with authors changing humanoids from Euclid to Safe to let them on Omega-7. It didn't happen with 105 out of universe, and it wasn't exactly rampant — people forget how few SCPs were supposedly on Omega-7 — but it was definitely a thing. I can't remember which SCPs this applied to; they may in fact all be deleted, but when I found that out, it stuck with me.)

When Omega-7 was wiped, many of the changes still stayed. Keep in mind, too, that different Sites often work like different 'fiefdoms', and may work off different classification systems.

Site-17, being the primary humanoid containment site, generally is used to working with multiple classification systems depending on who's 'in charge' of the SCP, and since her containment procedures wouldn't change anyway in the slightest, no one's wanted to do the paperwork wrangling and navigate surprise bureaucratic obstacles to change her (back) to Euclid. Why stick your neck out, politically, for such little benefit? (Or so the logic goes.)

item.title: Staff Post: Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded: Version 78 of this article has been reverted, as it made content edits without author permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My downvote seems to have disappeared. I would've removed it anyway.

I still can't upvote this in good conscience. It improved enough for me to not hate it, but something about the whole "Team Iris" schtick still makes me gag. Yes, we need to acknowledge that Omega-Seven happened as a reason we don't try to use SCP Objects anymore, but the piece doesn't feel as organic as when it was one team.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if she were to reach through a photo THROUGH A PHOTO!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, that's helpful. I never saw that ihp replied. I'm incorporating this now, with some edits, because it's better than what I wrote. Thanks! @ ihpkmn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry for the necro-post, but I have to say I absolutely love this, and want to see it in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just noticed that you said this over a month late. Derp.

Anyway. The main problem I have with the dialog in the interview is Iris's. She sounds like a robot trying to imitate an 18-year-old girl. This part in particular feels stilted as all hell:

I was twelve. After my family had taken a trip to the Grand Canyon, I was looking through the photo album, and I accidentally touched one and pushed a small rock over the edge in the photo. I thought it was my imagination at first, but after that I became a photography fanatic. I took pictures everywhere. I couldn't usually do anything with them, but for my thirteenth birthday, my parents got me a Polaroid camera. I loved that camera so much that I think it made things easier.

Here's an alternative suggestion:

SCP-105: It first happened when I was… eleven, twelve, maybe? My family took a trip to the Grand Canyon. I looked through the photo album after we got home, and brushed my hand up against one by accident. When I did, I pushed a rock over the edge, falling into the canyon; I could actually hear it clatter on the way down. (SCP-105 inhales and exhales deeply).

Dr. █████: Go on.

SCP-105: I thought it was my imagination at first, but after a while, I realized that it wasn't. I became fascinated with photography after that. Most of the time, it didn't work with photographs I took, but my parents got me a Polaroid One Step 600 camera- I had been begging for them to get it since Christmas. (SCP-105 starts smiling.) After I got the camera, the photos got… easier to interact with.

E: Reading Comprehension. I no has it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Objection: That's not a new problem. >:P

Suggestions, perchance?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While this is much more logical, and addresses several problems I had with it (particularly the "Why the fuck would not not demonstrate your ability?" thing), I have a new problem: the interview. Iris's dialog feels unrealistic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've done a bit of a rewrite on this after perusing comments. I am happy to change/add more.

Notable revisions:

  • All references to O5 orders removed.
  • Contradictory bits about 105's abilities regarding her personal camera / all cameras cleaned up.
  • Article slightly expanded to give more 'fluff', to alleviate the issue of it feeling 'sketchy'.
  • Illogical bits removed, such as the death row thing, the refusal to demonstrate abilities, etc.

A couple notes:

  • I do not feel this article would benefit from removing all indications of 105's personality. That wouldn't make people like it any better, but would remove peoples' reasons for liking this in the first place.
  • I am ambivalent on removing doofy shit like Team Able & Team Iris, mainly because I'm not sure what to replace it with and it does add flavor. It rings true to me that people could've come up with these informal names and they got leaked into documentation for <whatever reasons>.
  • I personally have zero ideas on how to describe her ability better; I tend to suck at that. Suggestions welcome.

In general I'm willing to make more edits based on reasonable suggestions (including additions that make it seem less 'sketchy'). Sorry I took so long to get to this.

Roget, why not go ahead and add the picture? Assuming it's CC, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hit me up this afternoon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clef has given me permission to do further cleanup, so feel free to get that picture if you're still up for it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to give Clef a picture of a polaroid camera, but neither of us followed up on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What "needless issues"?

  • "Image removed by O5 order" as opposed to simply not having an image (there should be justification if the O5s get involved, or it's wanky to try to pump up how significant the SCP is.)
  • O5 Council voting on her not coming in contact with her camera, as opposed to simply disallowing it.
  • Expecting the reader to believe that a cute [likely] young [and probably likeable] white teenage girl (whose story makes her sound like the definition of insanity defense) ended up on death row. This is nonsensical [it's incredibly, drastically, unlikely to happen in real life] and seems like another way to pump up how terrible~ her story is. To be blunt, it only happens in fanfic.
  • Plus, she's apparently unwilling to demonstrate her special powers even when her life is at stake, which is equally nonsensical. A better version would be that she was put on trial, demonstrated her anomalous ability to try to save her own life [realistically she'd never go on death row, but she could be afraid that's would happen], and then the Foundation snatched her and dosed everyone with amnestics.

The rest is significantly improved… as I already mentioned.

However, it's absurd to claim that this doesn't fix any problems from the previous article, or that it isn't a noticeable improvement.

You're right. Good thing I never, ever claimed that. Come on, now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get that Iris isn't really "how we do things now", and I can see why, but I still hope that this article sticks around for a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to disagree that it's an "improvement". But, my opinion's already on file a page or two up the thread, so I'll clam up now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What "needless issues"? Her powers have been nerfed severely, the overall tone of the article has been drastically improved, it focused a lot less on her personality, she has less privileges than before, the thing with the doctor was downgraded to a passing comment, and the entire point of Omega-7 is to say the exact same shit it's being criticized for.

I'm not saying this is perfect, or that it couldn't be better. However, it's absurd to claim that this doesn't fix any problems from the previous article, or that it isn't a noticeable improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually came back to look at this because Clef writing was being discussed…

…And I am disappointed to see that the needless issues are still present.

Clef, are you going to actually come back to this one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it's any consolation, the original 105 will probably be fashionable again in a year or two…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read all the original required reading and the majority of the guides. I guess I figured the original 100 somethin' where kinda of "The Exception Not The Rule" type dealies. Guess I walked in with that mentality, if it's outdated and mistaken, guess it's time to learn the new mentality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe take a look at the humanoid guide to get an idea of how humanoids are generally seen by today's standards?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read this way back in early 2010, and remembered thinking how neat this SCP was, particularly the Team-thing latter-
Trying to make SCPs actually useful seemed like a good idea- Now everyone seems to hate it it? Or is it the fact she's a humanoid AND a totly hawt teenage gurl guiz? I don't get it. Sad to see the article like this. Now it's just…Eh. Sad downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Her powers does NOT work that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This comment made me laugh harder than half the -Js. hahahaha

But really, that actually seems like a good measure to use when considering if you should put an "O5 approval required" in your article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If there's anything that exposes "no bad ideas" as a lie, it's this. Not the actual article, but the comments. There are many valid criticisms, but just as many posts that shit all over everything just because of the concept or having extremely elevated standards just because of the concept. Most notably, the comments about Omega-7 that add zero actual reasoning for hating it in its current state, and the X-men comments, despite her being easily separated from her pretty limited powers.

I'm not saying you can't dislike stuff, but seriously, either stop being biased against concepts or stop parroting that anything can be good. The two are completely contradictory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with waterfire. This article ties in to so many other places that a fundamental change is completely untennable.

Personally, I was fond of the original version. It was an article that tied into a larger segment of the foundationverse. The current edition feels like an abridged version, skimping on the details and requiring us to know the original to get the full story.

To all the people who are hating on Omega-7, I do not understand. I can get why the original thinking of o-7 is hated. It tended to idealize scps and was leading to a very different foundation than the one of today. But i really don;t understand what's so hated about its current place in the foundation's history. It indicates that at some point, people thought it was a good idea to weaponize scps, which if you've watched nearly any sci-fi movie ever, is a very common theme and absolutely something that would happen. Omega-7 serves to show how that idea can go terribly wrong, and helps cement the current thinking.
If you eliminate Omega-7, you get a MASSIVE inconsistency in the current foundation. Without it, you get a foundation that has never once tried to weaponize scps. Which means that when some researcher suggests it, nobody has anything to say against it. Omega-7 is currently what can be pointed to in order to say "THAT is why we don't weaponize them."

Also, does it seem strange to anyone else that the effects are now localized on a single camera, but her backstory is unchanged.

I'm in agreement on the O-5 requirement being excessive. it's not like giving her the camera is hugely dangerous. She can only access where she takes pictures of, and pictures could be confiscated. Proper proceedure would reduce the risk in her comning in contact with the camera to nil. Consider the thought of Experiment requests for 105 being next to reports detailing facility on-site nuke detonations on an O-5's desk. Does this seem reasonable?
